16368.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta is the 63rd sutta of the Khandha Saṃyutta (SN 22.63) and deals with the concept of upādiyamāna, i.e., the clinging or attachment that underlies the arising of phenomena.

50816.meaning_1: discourse on the mind of thought
50816.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry is the 64th sutta of Saṃyutta Nikāya 22. It is a kammadhāraya compound of maññamāna “thought‑mind” and sutta “discourse”, hence “discourse on the mind of thought”.

7789.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta title combines the present participle abhinandamāna “rejoicing, praising” with sutta “discourse”, indicating a discourse dealing with rejoicing or praise. It appears in the 22nd chapter of the Saṃyutta Nikāya.

7788.meaning_1: delighting (in); being pleased (with); finding pleasure (in); looking forward (to)
7788.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “rūpaṃ kho, bhante, abhinandamāno baddho mārassa”, abhinandamāna describes the bhikkhu as “delighted (in) form”, i.e., taking pleasure in form, which leads to being bound by Māra.
7788.example_1: “Sir, when you take pleasure in form you’re bound by Māra.

3756.meaning_1: not delighting (in); not being pleased (with); not finding pleasure (in); not looking forward (to)
3756.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “anabhinandamāno mutto pāpimato” the word is a present participle describing mutto, meaning “not delighted” or “free from pleasure”, i.e., the freedom is not accompanied by any delight in the wicked one.
3756.example_1: Not taking pleasure, you’re free from the Wicked One.”

3557.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the Anattaniya Sutta, the seventh sutta of the Khandha Samyutta (SN 22.69), a discourse dealing with the doctrine of non‑self.

54426.meaning_1: royal assembly discourse; discourse on the royal assembly
54426.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta is part of the Khandha Saṃyutta and deals with matters concerning a royal assembly; the compound follows the typical kammadhāraya pattern of an adjective (rajanīya) + past participle (saṇṭhita) + neuter noun (sutta).

54425.meaning_1: appearing desirable; with an enticing form
54425.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage the adjective rajanīyasaṇṭhitaṃ describes an object “shaped by arousal”, conveying the sense of something alluring or desirably formed.
54425.sutta_1: “yaṃ kho, bhikkhu, rajanīyasaṇṭhitaṃ; tatra te chando pahātabbo”ti.
54425.example_1: “Mendicant, give up desire for whatever is shaped by arousal.”

54994.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Entry completed based on typical sutta naming conventions; meaning derived from root rādha “sound”.

64358.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the Surādha Sutta (SN 22.72), a discourse named after the figure or term “surādha”. It is a neuter noun compound, classified as a kammadhāraya.

64356.family_set: names of monks; names of arahants
64356.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Surādha is a proper name of a monk, used vocatively in the sentence, indicating “Venerable Surādha”.
64356.sutta_1: atha kho āyasmā surādho bhagavantaṃ etadavoca:
64356.example_1: Then Venerable Surādha said to the Buddha:

3558.meaning_1: not belonging to a self; not related to a self; not one's own
3558.construction:
na + an + atta + īya
anatta + īya
3558.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of SN22.72 the adjective describes something that is “non‑self‑like” (anattanīya) and is used to qualify rajanīyasaṇṭhitaṃ, indicating a quality not belonging to any self.
3558.example_1:
upādiyamaññamānā,
athābhinandamāno ca,
aniccaṃ dukkhaṃ anattā ca,
anattanīyaṃ rajanīyasaṇṭhitaṃ,
rādhasurādhena te dasāti.

23294.meaning_1: the khajjanīya section; the digging section
23294.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.82:15.8 the term marks the third “khajjanīya” division of the Saṃyutta Nikāya, i.e., the third section dealing with digging or agricultural matters.

59995.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The Samudaya Sutta is the discourse on the origin of phenomena, listed as the second sutta in the Khandha Samyutta of SN 22.

33415.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
This is the second Udāya sutta in the Saṃyutta Nikāya, meaning “second Udāya discourse”.

53392.meaning_1: net of delusion; web of illusion
53392.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “mohajālaṃ padālitaṃ” the noun is used in the nominative singular to denote the “net of delusion” that is shattered, underscoring the metaphor of illusion being broken.
53392.example_1: and the net of delusion is shattered.

58050.meaning_1: roaming within the good qualities; having the seven good qualities as natural habitat
58050.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context “satta saddhammagocarā”, the term describes a domain or “pasture” that is naturally suited to the seven wholesome qualities (saddhammatā). It conveys the idea of an environment in which the good teachings are cultivated and “roamed”.
58050.example_1: their domain is the seven good qualities.

9861.trans: knowledge of an adept; knowledge of an arahant
9861.meaning_1: understanding of an adept; knowledge of an arahant
9861.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound combines the negated prefix a‑ (non‑) with sekha “skill, attainment” and ñāṇa “knowledge”, yielding the sense “knowledge of a perfected one (an arahant)”. In the sutta the form appears as asekhañāṇamuppannaṃ, indicating the arising of such knowledge.

31893.meaning_1: attained the level of mastery; epithet of an arahant
31893.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage the adjective dantabhūmimanuppattā describes an arahant who has reached the “tooth‑level” of mastery, i.e. the stage of the tamed. The compound consists of danta (tooth, metaphorically “tamed”), bhūmi (ground, level) and anuppatta (having attained).
31893.example_1: They’ve reached the level of the tamed,

33142.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The headword denotes the second Arahanta Sutta (discourse on the deathless) in the Khandha Saṃyutta of the Saṃyutta Nikāya. It is a neuter noun compound: dutiya (second) + arahanta (the deathless) + sutta (discourse).

63343.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term sīhasutta refers to the “Lion Discourse”, a sutta in the Khandhaka section of the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN 22.78).

31682.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context the word denotes a hole or pit, the place into which animals retreat; it is a masculine noun formed as a compound of daka “pit” and saya “place”.
31682.sutta_1: ye hi keci, bhikkhave, tiracchānagatā pāṇā sīhassa migarañño nadato saddaṃ suṇanti; yebhuyyena bhayaṃ saṃvegaṃ santāsaṃ āpajjanti; bilaṃ bilāsayā pavisanti; dakaṃ dakāsayā pavisanti; vanaṃ vanāsayā pavisanti; ākāsaṃ pakkhino bhajanti.
31682.example_1: And whatever animals hear the roar of the lion, king of beasts, are typically filled with fear, awe, and terror. They return to their lairs, be they in a hole, the water, or a wood; and the birds take to the air.

44843.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “dhammacakkaṃ pavattayi” the aorist “pavattayi” means “set the Dhamma‑wheel in motion”, i.e. caused to roll forward.
44843.example_1: in all the world with its gods,

58253.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound verb santāsamāpādi, formed from santāsa “fear” + āpā “to go/come”, conveys the experience of becoming afraid, as in bhītā santāsamāpāduṃ “are afraid and full of terror”.
58253.example_1: are afraid and full of terror,

23293.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta is part of the Khandha Saṃyutta, dealing with a grain‑seller; the name is a kammadhāraya compound of khajjanīya “grain‑seller” and sutta “discourse”.

56714.meaning_1: to condition; to make conditioned; to produce conditioned phenomena
56714.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound combines the accusative noun saṅkhatam “conditioned (thing)” with the verb abhisaṅkharoti “to make conditioned”. In the context it conveys the idea that conditioned phenomena are produced, i.e., “to condition”.
56714.example_1: Choices produce conditioned phenomena; that’s why they’re called ‘choices’.

18205.meaning_1: with mind liberated in such a way; with such kind of emancipated heart
18205.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context the phrase describes a mendicant whose mind is freed in this manner, thus the compound conveys “mind liberated thus”.
18205.sutta_1: evaṃvimuttacittaṃ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhuṃ saindā devā sabrahmakā sapajāpatikā ārakāva namassanti:
18205.example_1: When a mendicant’s mind is freed like this, the gods together with Indra, the Divinity, and the Progenitor worship them from afar:

46095.meaning_1: the discourse on the alms bowl
46095.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Piṇḍolyasutta is a sutta (discourse) found in the Khandha Saṃyutta (SN 22.80). The term is a compound of piṇḍola “alms‑bowl” and sutta “discourse”, thus the literal meaning is “discourse on the alms‑bowl”.

62446.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound sārajja (kingdom, realm) + māna (pride, dignity) + rūpa (form) literally denotes a “form of royal pride”. In the context of the sutta it functions adverbially, conveying the sense “timidly, fearfully”.
62446.sutta_1: nisajja kho bhagavā tathārūpaṃ iddhābhisaṅkhāraṃ abhisaṅkhāsi yathā te bhikkhū ekadvīhikāya sārajjamānarūpā yenāhaṃ tenupasaṅkameyyuṃ.
62446.example_1: Then he used his psychic power to will that the mendicants would come to him timidly, alone or in pairs.

19382.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context it denotes a piece of wood that cannot be used for building, i.e., timber.
19382.sutta_1: seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, chavālātaṃ ubhatopadittaṃ majjhe gūthagataṃ, neva gāme kaṭṭhatthaṃ pharati, nāraññe kaṭṭhatthaṃ pharati.
19382.example_1: Suppose there was a firebrand for lighting a funeral pyre, burning at both ends, and smeared with dung in the middle. It couldn’t be used as timber either in the village or the wilderness.

45860.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term is a sutta name; it is a kammadhāraya compound meaning “discourse on the Pāli‑leyya”. It belongs to the Khandha Saṃyutta (SN 22).

45811.meaning_1: name of a town near Kosambī
45811.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN22.81 the Buddha stays at pārileyyaka, a settlement close to Kosambī; the term is a neuter noun used as a proper place name.
45811.example_1: atha kho bhagavā anupubbena cārikaṃ caramāno yena pālileyyakaṃ [pārileyyakaṃ] tadavasari. tatra sudaṃ bhagavā pālileyyake viharati bhaddasālamūle.

67415.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Used as an indeclinable adverb meaning “through investigation” or “analytically” in the sutta, derived from the noun vicaya with the taddhita suffix‑so.

29565.meaning_1: that which is born of that; conditioned
29565.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “tatojo so saṅkhāro” the word functions as an adjective meaning “that which is born of that”, i.e. a conditioned phenomenon.
29565.example_1: That conditioned phenomenon is born from that.

61280.meaning_1: eternalism; doctrine of an everlasting soul
61280.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.81:10.8 the term sassatadiṭṭhi denotes the “eternalist view” (the doctrine that a soul persists forever). It is a karmadhāraya compound of sassati “eternal” + diṭṭhi “view”, functioning as a feminine noun.
61280.sutta_1: yā kho pana sā, bhikkhave, sassatadiṭṭhi saṅkhāro so.
61280.example_1: But that eternalist view is just a conditioned phenomenon.

4184.grammar: fem, abstr, comp, from na niṭṭhaṅgata
4184.meaning_1: fact of not having come to a conclusion; fact of having not reached surety; fact of being in doubt (about)
4184.meaning_lit: not gone to the end state
4184.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
An abstract feminine noun meaning ‘the state of not having reached a conclusion, i.e., doubt’. Formed from the negative prefix a‑, the past‑participle niṭṭhaṅgata ‘not settled’, and the suffix –tā, and appears in the locative in the passage.
4184.sutta_1: yā kho pana sā, bhikkhave, kaṅkhitā vicikicchitā aniṭṭhaṅgatatā saddhamme saṅkhāro so.
4184.example_1: That doubt and uncertainty, the indecision about the true teaching, is just a conditioned phenomenon.

46608.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term puṇṇama means “bright” or “shining”; thus puṇṇamasutta is the discourse on brightness within the Khandhasutta section of the Saṃyutta Nikāya.

19647.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage katamatta is an interrogative compound meaning “which self?” or “which individual?” It combines the interrogative pronoun katama ‘which’ with the noun atta ‘self’. The form appears in the vocative singular (katamatto) and is used to question the identity of the self in the analysis of anattā.
19647.example_1: tena kho pana samayena aññatarassa bhikkhuno evaṃ cetaso parivitakko udapādi, iti kira bho rūpaṃ anattā, vedanā… saññā… saṅkhārā… viññāṇaṃ anattā, anattakatāni kammāni kathamattānaṃ [katamattānaṃ, kammattānaṃ] phusissantīti. atha kho bhagavā tassa bhikkhuno cetasā ceto parivitakkamaññāya bhikkhū āmantesi –

44290.meaning_1: understanding the train of thought; comprehending the thought process
44290.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of SN 22.82:12.4 the gerund indicates the Buddha’s act of understanding the monk’s train of thought before addressing him.
44290.sutta_1: atha kho bhagavā tassa bhikkhuno cetasā ceto parivitakkamaññāya bhikkhū āmantesi:
44290.example_1: Then the Buddha, knowing that monk’s train of thought, addressed the mendicants:

32072.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “ete dasavidhā vuttā” the adjective “dasavidha” qualifies “vuttā” (questions), meaning “ten kinds” – i.e., the ten questions listed.

23291.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “sīho khajjanī piṇḍolyaṃ”, khajjanī is a present‑participle adjective meaning “that which is being chewed”, describing the morsel (piṇḍolyaṃ).

45857.meaning_1: name of a town near Kosambī
45857.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Used instrumentally in the sentence “pālileyyena puṇṇamāti”, indicating the town of Pālileyya (near Kosambī) as the location being described as beautiful.

31630.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the citation “theravaggo catuttho” the term refers to the fourth vagga (chapter) of the Theragatha, i.e., the fourth section of the collection of verses of the elder monks.
